HackerNews.title: Agentic Coding Is a Trap
HackerNews.content:
# Agentic Coding is a Trap

### Remaining vigilant about cognitive debt and atrophy.


"AI does the coding, and the human in the loop is the orchestrator"

This is the sentiment being hyped up around the industry currently: traditional coding is all but dead, and Spec Driven Development (SDD) is the future. You generate a plan, and disconnect from writing any code. The agents know better, and handle all the implementation. You are there as *the expert*, to provide "good taste", review the outputs, and constantly steer the agent(s) to execute the plan that you meticulously put together.

The workflow takes many shapes at this point, but in general, it is a process where someone defines the project's requirements (simultaneously at a micro *and* macro level), generates a plan, and then [pulls the slot machine lever](https://blog.quent.in/blog/2026/03/09/one-more-prompt-the-dopamine-trap-of-agentic-coding/) over and over, iterating and reiterating with often *multiple* agent instances until it's done. All the while, putting a growing distance between the "orchestrator" and the code that is being generated and committed.

Coding Agents are helpful, and powerful, but there's already some quantifiable trade-offs that need to be discussed:

- An increase in the complexity of the surrounding systems to mitigate the increased ambiguity of AI's non-determinism.
- Atrophying skills for a wide swath of the population.
- Vendor lock-in for individuals and entire teams (Claude Code outages have already had entire teams at a stand-still).
- Fluctuating and increasing costs to access the tools. An employee's cost is fixed; tokens are a constantly moving target.

Being successful with this approach to coding agents hinges on a rather crucial element: only a skilled developer who's thinking critically, and comfortable operating at the architectural level, can spot issues in the thousands of lines of generated code, *before* they become a problem.

Yet, in an ironic twist of fate, it's the individual's critical thinking skills and cognitive clarity that AI tooling has now been proven to [impact negatively](https://margaretstorey.com/blog/2026/02/18/cognitive-debt-revisited/).

Not Just Another "Abstraction"

A common refrain we hear in the community is that programmers are just "moving up the stack" and into a different type of abstraction. Whether or not these tools are really an abstraction layer in the first place is not a settled matter; a higher level of ambiguity is not a higher level of abstraction.

If we put that to the side though, it is true that programmers tend to be wary of new languages and new ways of programming. When FORTRAN was released, programmers were skeptical of it, too. They had similar claims: it was likely to introduce more bugs and instability, and writing assembly directly was more efficient. Later, there would be discourse around the integration of compilers introducing too much "magic" into the process. These were normative arguments around a fear of what *might* be lost if these new technologies were embraced.

The difference with what is happening today is that those previous fears were speculative and theoretical. In just the short few years that AI tooling has existed, we are already seeing significant impacts. These aren't *just junior developers*, but even those with a decade (or more) of experience:

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-06.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-05.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-02.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-01.png)

Junior developers are faced with an even steeper climb, as we truncate their ability to work with code and replace it with reviewing generated code. Reviewing code is important, but it's only 50% of the learning process, at best. Without the friction and challenges that come with working with code directly, their ability to learn is seriously diminished.

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-04.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-07.png)

Studying this phenomenon takes time, so anecdotal evidence is important to gather to get a real-time view of the situation. But it has also been studied, and there are [numerous](https://www.media.mit.edu/publications/your-brain-on-chatgpt/) [reports](https://www.404media.co/microsoft-study-finds-ai-makes-human-cognition-atrophied-and-unprepared-3/) reinforcing that this is a real phenomenon.

It actually is different this time.

When a C++ developer moved to Java or Python, they didn't complain of brain fog. When a sysadmin moved to AWS, they didn't feel like they were losing their ability to understand networking.

A Senior Engineer losing their coding edge and becoming "rusty" over time as they move into managerial roles and practice coding less is not a new phenomenon. This was the natural progression of expertise: an engineer who had **decades** of coding, friction, and experience logged would have the time and experience to solidify those skills and wisdom. And they could apply that wisdom when their job became less about syntax, and more about higher-level architectural decisions. Those individuals are not only exceedingly rare, but you won't get the next wave of seniors if we're all abdicating the friction of writing, problem-solving, and debugging.

What is happening right now is a trend where developers, who've never had that longevity or the 30+ years of friction that led to that deep understanding, are being moved into higher-level workflows requiring the same skills to manage the AI agents that the senior engineer took decades to obtain.

However, Senior Engineers aren't immune, either. [Simon Willison](https://simonwillison.net/2026/Feb/15/cognitive-debt/), a developer with nearly 30 years experience, has reported not having a *"firm mental model of what the applications can do and how they work, which means each additional feature becomes harder to reason about"*

The "Skilled" Orchestrator Problem

Buried in a recent [study](https://www.anthropic.com/research/how-ai-is-transforming-work-at-anthropic#and-less-hands-on-practice) by Anthropic was a surprisingly honest moment when speaking about the risks of engaging with coding agents on a regular basis:

One reason that the atrophy of coding skills is concerning is the “paradox of supervision” ... effectively using Claude requires supervision, and supervising Claude requires the very coding skills that

may atrophy from AI overuse.

Sandor Nyako, [Director of Software Engineering at LinkedIn](https://www.businessinsider.com/leaders-worry-about-skill-atrophy-due-to-ai-adoption-2025-10#8786254a-3fe1-4407-98a2-7c2eaac66b6f) who oversees 50 engineers, has noticed it proliferating throughout the organization and requested his team not to use them for *"tasks that require critical thinking or problem-solving."*

"To grow skills, people need to go through hardship. They need to develop the muscle to think through problems," he said. "How would someone question if AI is accurate if they don't have critical thinking?"


There is also the question of what constitutes "overuse". We already have evidence, both [data-driven](https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-assistance-coding-skills) and [anecdotal](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pzkwn3hu1Cc), that these skills can atrophy and dissipate rather quickly (within months in some cases).

This is the contradiction that has many AI boosters talking out of both sides of their mouths: The use of coding agents is actively diminishing the very skills needed to effectively manage the coding agents.

LLMs accelerate the wrong parts.

Contrary to the current narrative that is being espoused, we didn’t necessarily *need* to write code faster. Especially code we didn’t fully understand, and particularly in huge swaths that we couldn't review in reasonable time frames.

Before AI, a (good) developer's priority list might look like:

- Understanding of the code and its relation to the codebase
- If the code is aligned with the documented and efficient standards
- As few lines of code as needed to accomplish the goal (while maintaining readability)
- Turnaround time

*completely invert this list*. Agentic coding, and LLMs in general,

*completely invert this list*.

Their capabilities and usage tend to focus on speed by increasing the amount of code that can be generated in a specified time frame. Speed is a natural byproduct of high aptitude. When it's forced, it always leads to lower accuracy. The integration of these tools doesn't tend to focus much on deeper understanding or conciseness.

Can they be used that way? Yes, with determination, they certainly can be.

Are they? No, not really; forced mandates and [hype around token usage across organizations](https://www.pymnts.com/artificial-intelligence-2/2026/ai-adoption-is-being-measured-in-tokens-but-the-metric-falls-short-experts-say/) is demonstrating as such.

Coding === Planning

There is a divide between developers that isn’t highlighted as much: *Some of us plan, and think, better with code*. Thinking and working in code isn't just meaningless drudgery; it forces you to think about things on a technical level that involves everything from security to performance to user experience to maintainability.

In a recent [interview](https://youtu.be/IGsbARhERqc?t=501) discussing "Spec Driven Development", Dax, the creator of OpenCode *(an open-source coding agent, no less)* was quoted saying:

“When working on something new or something challenging,

me typing out code is the process by which I figure out what we should even be doing.

I have a really tough time just sitting there, writing out a giant spec on exactly how the feature should work.

I like writing out types. I like writing out how some of the functions might play together. I like playing with folder structure to see what the different concepts should be. And this is all stuff that I think most people—most programmers—have always done. I don't really see a good reason why I would stop that personally, because it's how I figure out what to do.”

What you *say* is often not what you *mean*, and LLMs fill in ambiguity with assumptions (or hallucinations), which leads to: **more** review, **more** agent revisions, **more** tokens burned, and **more** disconnection from what is being created. Inversely, You can marvel at the most beautiful, unambiguous, perfectly structured prompt you've ever written, and the LLM can still output a hallucinated method because it is fundamentally a next-token-prediction engine, not a compiler. You cannot replace a deterministic system with a probabilistic one and expect zero ambiguity.

Even the most [AI-enthusiastic senior developers](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cv6rwHTGT5w) are starting to see this disconnection as a looming and growing issue.

Vendor Lock-In

When I was browsing LinkedIn during the Claude outage that occurred a bit ago, I noticed numerous [posts](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/claudes-outages-show-dark-side-ai-productivity-total-system-lam-osbjc/) highlighting that certain developers and engineering teams were at a standstill. Their workflows, their own coding abilities, had *already* reached a point where they were largely dependent on these vendors. What used to be a skill that they could execute with just a keyboard and text editor suddenly required a subscription to an AI model provider.

You can't predict your token cost.

Model providers are [heavily subsidized](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/917380/ai-monetization-anthropic-openai-token-economics-revenue), and the models themselves are built on shifting sands. Every new model release follows the same pattern of high benchmarks, followed by hype, followed by the reality of usage and everyone complaining of them being "nerfed" and burning through 2x-3x as many tokens to get the same job done.

You *know* how much your employees cost; you have *no idea* how much your token costs will be day to day, month to month, year to year. If your entire team is using agentic coding as the default, your expense account will need to remain highly nimble. As Primeagen said recently: *"when you use these fully agentic workflows, the model providers essentially own you".*

It's not unreasonable to play this pattern forward, where we could be creating an industry where you *need* to pay for token consumption to accomplish something that used to be the product of your own critical thinking and problem-solving abilities. This would resemble a type of "vendor lock-in", but for an entire industry skillset (and I'm sure the model providers are gleefully [rubbing their hands](https://giphy.com/gifs/giphyqa-g0JP0HG6zF0o8) in anticipation for that). The financial, and intellectual, rug-pull could come at any moment, and local LLMs are nowhere near ready to scale to absorb that level of usage.

This isn't theoretical conjecture; [it's being reported on right now](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5HaQnIPrfKk). Even the model providers themselves are bringing it to light. Yet another Anthropic [study](https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-assistance-coding-skills) showed a precipitous **47% drop-off** in debugging skills:

“Incorporating AI aggressively into the workplace—especially in software engineering—inevitably comes with trade-offs...developers may lean on AI to deliver quick results at the expense of building critical skills—most notably, the ability to debug when things go wrong.”


There's a way to avoid all of this, of course. LLMs are a powerhouse technological advancement, and when used responsibly, they can be a stellar tool for learning and upskilling. They enable me to dive deeper and wider into concepts and techniques, expanding understanding and enabling exploration of new ideas that used to be more arduous and time consuming to experiment with. This is where I think they will offer the industry the most long-term value.

My Approach: Demote AI's role

I'm certainly not advocating for typing code out manually. Programmers have always been looking for ways to *create* code without having to *write* code. This is why we even have [Emmet](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/emmet), autocomplete, and snippets in the first place. Even COBOL was designed to encapsulate more instructions with less writing by using "English-like" words such as `MOVE`

and `WRITE`

. jQuery's motto was ["write less, do more"](https://brand.jquery.org/logos/). LLMs are another addition to this array of code generation tools.

What I am advocating for, though, is leveraging LLMs and coding agents as secondary processes. A way that doesn't sacrifice the individual's skills at the altar of productivity. You can flip the script and lean on them to brainstorm the planning parts of the process while staying actively engaged throughout implementation, delegating to them on an as-needed basis. You can leverage the productivity gains, and mitigate the comprehension debt.

My daily workflow:

- I use LLMs to help generate specs and plans, while
*I facilitate the implementation*. This is an inversion of the "orchestration" workflow; I am still manually coding anywhere from 20% to 100%, depending on the task. - I very often am writing pseudo-code when I do engage with the models, closing the distance between the request and the generated code.
- I use the models as
[delegation utilities](https://cheewebdevelopment.com/dont-vibe-code-delegate-responsible-development-with-llms/)for ad-hoc code generation and interactive documentation, as well as research tools so that I can constantly ask questions, iterate, refactor, and gain clarity around my approaches. *I never generate more than I can review in a sitting*. If it's too much to review, I slow down and split the task up, manually refactoring where needed to ensure a comprehensive understanding of the end result.- I never ask an LLM or agent to implement something that I've never done before or couldn't do on my own, except perhaps purely for educational or tutorial purposes (and often discarded afterwards).

If I had to TL;DR this list, it would be: Use them like the Ship's Computer, not Data. *(any Star Trek fans should get the reference)*

I'm not going faster, but I'm doing better quality work.

The productivity gains from these models are real, *and so is the friction and understanding that come from engaging with the work on a tangible and frequent basis*.

Despite the countless failed attempts at trying to democratize coding while not understanding coding, we're faced with the reality that you **cannot** understand code without engaging with it. And it's become clear that if you don't keep engaging and writing it, you *can* lose touch with that understanding, which will in turn make you a less capable orchestrator in the first place, rendering this phase of AI coding a strange and needlessly stressful interlude.

Perhaps I am worrying too much, but history contains lessons.

This all feels similar, though, like another large experiment we're running on ourselves. We've been through a similar period with the introduction of social media without understanding the long-term implications, and we're now faced with attention deficit (amongst many other issues) on a wide scale.

This time, we're gambling with something much riskier.

“People who go all in on AI agents now are guaranteeing their obsolescence. If you outsource all your thinking to computers, you stop upskilling, learning, and becoming more competent.”



– Jeremy Howard, creator of[fast.ai]
HackerNews.author: ayoisaiah
HackerNews.chinese_content:
# Agentic Coding 是一个陷阱

### 对认知债务和能力萎缩保持警惕

"AI 负责编码，人类作为监督者进行协调"

这是目前行业内被大肆炒作的观点：传统编码已近乎消亡，规范驱动开发（Spec Driven Development, SDD）才是未来。你生成一份计划，然后不再编写任何代码。智能体（agent）更懂行，它们处理所有实现工作。你作为*专家*的角色，负责提供"良好品味"、审查输出，并不断引导智能体执行你精心制定的计划。

目前这种工作流程有多种形式，但总体而言，它是一个这样的过程：有人定义项目需求（同时在微观*和*宏观层面），生成一份计划，然后一次又一次地[拉动老虎机摇杆](https://blog.quent.in/blog/2026/03/09/one-more-prompt-the-dopamine-trap-of-agentic-coding/)，反复迭代，通常需要*多个*智能体实例协同工作，直到任务完成。而在这个过程中，"协调者"与被生成和提交的代码之间的距离越来越大。

编码智能体（Coding Agent）确实有用且强大，但已经出现了一些需要讨论的可量化权衡：

- 周边系统的复杂性增加，以应对 AI 非确定性带来的更高模糊性。
- 大量人群的技能正在萎缩。
- 个人和整个团队面临供应商锁定风险（Claude Code 的宕机已导致整个团队工作停滞）。
- 使用这些工具的成本波动且不断上升。员工的成本是固定的；而 token 的成本则是一个不断变化的目标。

通过这种方式成功使用编码智能体，取决于一个相当关键的因素：只有那些具备批判性思维、能够在架构层面自如运作的熟练开发者，才能在数千行生成的代码中，*在问题出现之前*就发现隐患。

然而，命运的讽刺之处在于，AI 工具已被证明会[对个人的批判性思维能力和认知清晰度产生负面影响](https://margaretstorey.com/blog/2026/02/18/cognitive-debt-revisited/)。

## 不仅仅是另一种"抽象"

我们在社区中经常听到一种说法：程序员只是在"向上迁移"，进入一种不同类型的抽象。这些工具是否真的算作一个抽象层，本身尚无定论；更高的模糊性并不等同于更高层次的抽象。

不过，暂且搁置这一点，程序员对新语言和新编程方式持谨慎态度确实是事实。当 FORTRAN 发布时，程序员也曾对其持怀疑态度。他们提出了类似的观点：它可能引入更多错误和不稳定性，而直接编写汇编语言更高效。后来，关于编译器集成在过程中引入了太多"魔法"的讨论也出现了。这些都是围绕如果接受这些新技术*可能*会失去什么而产生的规范性争论。

与今天正在发生的事情不同之处在于，过去的那些担忧是推测性和理论性的。而在 AI 工具存在的短短几年里，我们已经看到了显著的影响。这*不仅仅是初级开发者*，甚至包括那些拥有十年（或更多）经验的人：

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-06.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-05.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-02.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-01.png)

初级开发者面临着更陡峭的攀登，因为我们削弱了他们直接处理代码的能力，取而代之的是审查生成的代码。审查代码固然重要，但充其量只占学习过程的 50%。没有直接与代码打交道所带来的摩擦和挑战，他们的学习能力会严重下降。

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-04.png)

![Coding Agents Paradox](/skills-07.png)

研究这一现象需要时间，因此收集轶事证据对于实时了解情况很重要。但这一现象也已被研究，有[大量](https://www.media.mit.edu/publications/your-brain-on-chatgpt/)[报告](https://www.404media.co/microsoft-study-finds-ai-makes-human-cognition-atrophied-and-unprepared-3/)证实这是一个真实存在的现象。

## 这次确实不一样

当 C++ 开发者转向 Java 或 Python 时，他们不会抱怨思维模糊。当系统管理员迁移到 AWS 时，他们不会觉得自己正在失去理解网络的能力。

一位资深工程师在转向管理岗位、减少编码实践后，随着时间的推移逐渐失去编码优势、变得"生疏"，这并不是一个新现象。这是专业知识的自然演进：一位拥有**数十年**编码、摩擦和经验积累的工程师，会有足够的时间和经验来巩固这些技能和智慧。当他们的工作不再那么关注语法，而更多关注更高层次的架构决策时，他们可以运用这些智慧。但这样的人不仅极其罕见，而且如果我们都放弃了编写代码、解决问题和调试过程中的摩擦，就不会有下一波资深工程师涌现。

目前正在发生的趋势是，那些从未拥有过那种长期积累或 30 多年摩擦所带来深刻理解的开发者，正在被推入需要同样技能来管理 AI 智能体的更高层次工作流程——而这些技能正是资深工程师花费数十年才获得的。

然而，资深工程师也并非免疫。[Simon Willison](https://simonwillison.net/2026/Feb/15/cognitive-debt/)，一位拥有近 30 年经验的开发者，报告称自己不再拥有*"对应用程序能做什么以及如何工作的清晰心智模型，这意味着每个新功能都变得更难推理"*。

## "熟练"协调者的问题

Anthropic 近期的一项[研究](https://www.anthropic.com/research/how-ai-is-transforming-work-at-anthropic#and-less-hands-on-practice)中，在谈及频繁使用编码代理（coding agent）的风险时，出现了一个令人意外且坦诚的时刻：

编码技能退化的一个令人担忧之处在于“监督悖论”……有效使用 Claude 需要监督，而监督 Claude 又恰恰需要那些可能因过度使用 AI 而退化的编码技能。

LinkedIn 的[软件工程总监](https://www.businessinsider.com/leaders-worry-about-skill-atrophy-due-to-ai-adoption-2025-10#8786254a-3fe1-4407-98a2-7c2eaac66b6f) Sandor Nyako 管理着 50 名工程师，他已注意到这种现象在整个组织内蔓延，并要求他的团队不要将编码代理用于 *“需要批判性思维或问题解决能力的任务”*。

“要提升技能，人们需要经历困难。他们需要锻炼思考问题的能力，”他说，“如果一个人没有批判性思维，又怎能质疑 AI 的准确性呢？”

此外，还有一个问题：什么才算是“过度使用”？我们已有[数据驱动](https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-assistance-coding-skills)和[轶事](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pzkwn3hu1Cc)两方面的证据表明，这些技能可能在短时间内（某些情况下仅需数月）退化并迅速消失。

这正是许多 AI 鼓吹者自相矛盾之处：编码代理的使用正在削弱有效管理编码代理所必需的技能。

LLM（大语言模型）加速了错误的部分。

与当前流行的说法相反，我们未必 *需要* 更快地编写代码。尤其是那些我们并不完全理解的代码，以及那些我们无法在合理时间内审查的大段代码。

在 AI 出现之前，一个（优秀）开发者的优先级列表可能是这样的：

- 理解代码及其与代码库的关系
- 代码是否符合文档化和高效的标准
- 用尽可能少的代码行数实现目标（同时保持可读性）
- 交付周期

而代理式编码（agentic coding）以及 LLM（大语言模型）整体上，*完全颠覆了这个列表*。

它们的能力和用法往往侧重于速度，即在指定时间内生成更多代码。速度本是高能力的自然副产品，但当它被强行追求时，总会导致准确率下降。这些工具的集成通常并不注重更深层次的理解或代码的简洁性。

它们能这样用吗？可以，只要有决心，当然可以。

它们被这样用了吗？不，并没有；强制性的要求以及[各组织对 token 使用量的炒作](https://www.pymnts.com/artificial-intelligence-2/2026/ai-adoption-is-being-measured-in-tokens-but-the-metric-falls-short-experts-say/)已经证明了这一点。

编码 === 规划

开发者之间存在一个未被充分强调的分歧：*我们中的一些人，通过编码来更好地规划和思考*。用代码进行思考和工作并非毫无意义的苦差事；它迫使你在技术层面思考问题，涉及从安全性、性能、用户体验到可维护性等方方面面。

在近期一次讨论“规范驱动开发”（Spec Driven Development）的[采访](https://youtu.be/IGsbARhERqc?t=501)中，OpenCode *（一个开源编码代理）* 的创建者 Dax 被引述道：

“在处理全新或具有挑战性的工作时，我敲出代码的过程，就是我弄清楚我们究竟应该做什么的过程。

我很难只是坐在那里，写出一份关于某个功能应该如何运作的巨细无遗的规范。

我喜欢写类型定义。我喜欢思考一些函数如何协同工作。我喜欢摆弄文件夹结构，看看不同的概念应该是什么。我认为这些是大多数人——大多数程序员——一直在做的事情。我个人看不出有什么好理由要停止这样做，因为这就是我弄清楚该做什么的方式。”

你 *说* 的往往不是你 *想* 的，而 LLM（大语言模型）会用假设（或幻觉）来填补歧义，这导致：**更多**的审查、**更多**的代理修订、**更多**的 token 消耗，以及**更多**与所创建内容的脱节。反过来，你可能会为自己写出的最完美、最无歧义、结构最清晰的提示词（prompt）而惊叹，但 LLM（大语言模型）仍然可能输出一个幻觉出来的方法，因为它本质上是一个下一个 token 预测引擎（next-token-prediction engine），而不是一个编译器。你不能用一个概率系统取代一个确定性系统，却期望零歧义。

即使是[最热衷于 AI 的高级开发者](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cv6rwHTGT5w)也开始将这种脱节视为一个迫在眉睫且日益严重的问题。

供应商锁定（Vendor Lock-In）

当我在不久前 Claude 宕机期间浏览 LinkedIn 时，我注意到大量[帖子](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/claudes-outages-show-dark-side-ai-productivity-total-system-lam-osbjc/)指出，某些开发者和工程团队陷入了停滞。他们的工作流程，他们自己的编码能力，*已经*达到了一个严重依赖这些供应商的程度。曾经只需要一个键盘和文本编辑器就能执行的技能，突然之间就需要订阅某个 AI 模型提供商。

你无法预测你的 token 成本。

模型提供商[获得了大量补贴](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/917380/ai-monetization-anthropic-openai-token-economics-revenue)，而模型本身也建立在流沙之上。每一次新模型的发布都遵循同样的模式：高基准测试分数，接着是炒作，然后是实际使用中的现实，以及所有人抱怨模型被“削弱”，并且为了完成同样的工作需要消耗 2 到 3 倍的 token。

你*知道*员工成本是多少；但你*完全不知道*每天的 token（令牌）费用会是多少，更别提每月、每年了。如果你的整个团队都将智能体编码（agentic coding）作为默认工作方式，那么你的费用账户必须保持高度灵活。正如 Primeagen 最近所说：*“当你使用这些全智能体工作流时，模型提供商基本上就掌控了你。”*

顺着这个模式推演下去并非不合理：我们可能正在创造一个行业，在这个行业里，你*需要*为 token 消耗付费，才能完成那些曾经是你自身批判性思维和问题解决能力产出的工作。这有点像一种“供应商锁定”（vendor lock-in），但针对的是整个行业的技能集（而且我敢肯定，模型提供商正乐不可支地[搓着手](https://giphy.com/gifs/giphyqa-g0JP0HG6zF0o8)期待那一天的到来）。财务上和智力上的“抽地毯式”打击随时可能发生，而本地 LLM（大语言模型）远未准备好扩展到足以吸收那种级别的使用量。

这不是理论上的推测；[现在就有报道](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5HaQnIPrfKk)。甚至连模型提供商自己也在揭示这一点。Anthropic 的另一项[研究](https://www.anthropic.com/research/AI-assistance-coding-skills)显示，调试技能出现了**47% 的急剧下降**：

“将 AI 激进地融入工作场所——尤其是在软件工程领域——不可避免地会带来权衡……开发者可能会依赖 AI 来快速交付结果，却以牺牲关键技能的培养为代价——最值得注意的是，当事情出错时进行调试的能力。”

当然，有一种方法可以避免这一切。LLM 是一项强大的技术进步，如果负责任地使用，它们可以成为学习和提升技能的绝佳工具。它们让我能够更深入、更广泛地探索概念和技术，扩展理解，并探索那些过去尝试起来更费力、更耗时的全新想法。我认为，这才是它们能为行业带来最长期价值的地方。

**我的方法：降低 AI 的角色**

我当然不是在主张手动逐字输入代码。程序员一直在寻找*创造*代码而无需*编写*代码的方法。这正是我们首先拥有 [Emmet](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/emmet)、自动补全和代码片段（snippets）的原因。就连 COBOL 的设计初衷也是通过使用 `MOVE` 和 `WRITE` 这样的“类英语”词汇，用更少的书写来封装更多的指令。jQuery 的座右铭是[“写得更少，做得更多”](https://brand.jquery.org/logos/)。LLM 只是这一系列代码生成工具中的新成员。

然而，我所倡导的是，将 LLM 和编码智能体（coding agents）作为次要流程来利用。这是一种不会以牺牲个人技能为代价来换取生产力的方式。你可以反转剧本，在流程的规划阶段依靠它们进行头脑风暴，同时在实现过程中保持积极参与，按需委派任务。这样，你既能利用生产力提升，又能减轻理解债务（comprehension debt）。

**我的日常工作流程：**

- 我使用 LLM 来帮助生成规格和计划，而*我主导实现过程*。这是对“编排”（orchestration）工作流的反转；根据任务的不同，我仍然手动编码 20% 到 100% 的内容。
- 在与模型交互时，我经常编写伪代码（pseudo-code），以缩短请求与生成代码之间的距离。
- 我将模型用作[委派工具](https://cheewebdevelopment.com/dont-vibe-code-delegate-responsible-development-with-llms/)，用于临时代码生成和交互式文档，同时也作为研究工具，以便我能不断提问、迭代、重构，并理清我的方法思路。*我从不生成超过我一次能审查完的代码量。* 如果代码量太大无法一次审查，我会放慢速度，拆分任务，并在需要时手动重构，以确保对最终结果有全面的理解。
- 我从不要求 LLM 或智能体实现我从未做过或无法独立完成的事情，除非纯粹出于教育或教程目的（并且之后通常会丢弃）。

如果非要用一句话总结这份清单，那就是：把它们当作星舰的计算机来用，而不是 Data（数据人）。（*任何《星际迷航》粉丝都应该明白这个梗*）

我没有变得更快，但我的工作质量更高了。

这些模型带来的生产力提升是真实的，*而频繁、切实地参与工作所带来的摩擦和理解也同样真实*。

尽管在不懂编程的情况下试图让编程民主化的尝试无数次失败，但我们面临着一个现实：如果不参与其中，你**无法**理解代码。而且已经很清楚的是，如果你不再持续参与和编写代码，你*可能*会失去那种理解力，这反过来会让你首先成为一个能力更差的编排者，从而使 AI 编码的这个阶段变成一段奇怪且不必要地充满压力的插曲。

也许我担心过度了，但历史中蕴含着教训。

不过，这一切感觉很像另一个我们正在自己身上进行的重大实验。我们曾经历过类似时期，在社交媒体引入时并不了解其长期影响，而现在我们面临着大范围的注意力缺陷（以及许多其他问题）。

这一次，我们赌的是风险更大的东西。

“那些现在全力投入 AI 智能体的人，正在确保自己的过时。如果你把所有思考都外包给计算机，你就会停止提升技能、学习和变得更有能力。”

—— Jeremy Howard，[fast.ai](http://fast.ai) 创始人
HackerNews.chinese_title: 智能体编码是陷阱：警惕认知债务与技能萎缩
HackerNews.chinese_summary: 本文警告，当前被大肆炒作的“智能体编码”（Agentic Coding）模式——人类仅作为监督者，AI负责全部编码实现——正在导致严重的认知债务和技能退化。研究表明，过度依赖AI编码工具会削弱开发者的批判性思维、调试能力（下降47%）和系统理解力，甚至资深工程师也报告心智模型模糊。文章指出，这种模式还带来供应商锁定风险（如Claude宕机导致团队停滞）和不可控的Token成本，最终可能让整个行业陷入智力与财务的双重依赖陷阱。作者呼吁将AI作为辅助工具而非替代品，保持对编码过程的主动参与。

"Agentic Coding Is a Trap"
"AI tools have been shown to negatively impact an individual's critical thinking skills and cognitive clarity."
"The irony of fate is that the very skills needed to effectively manage coding agents are being eroded by their use."

HackerNews.title: OpenAI’s o1 correctly diagnosed 67% of ER patients vs. 50-55% by triage doctors
HackerNews.content:
From George Clooney in ER to Noah Wyle in The Pitt, emergency department doctors have long been popular heroes. But will it soon be time to hang up the scrubs?

A groundbreaking Harvard study has found that AI systems outperformed human doctors in high-pressure emergency medicine triage, diagnosing more accurately in the potentially life and death moments when people are first rushed to hospital.

The [results](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adz4433) were described by independent experts as showing “a genuine step forward” in the clinical reasoning of AIs and came as part of trials that tested the responses of hundreds of doctors against an AI.

The authors said the results, published in the journal Science, showed large language models (LLMs) “have eclipsed most benchmarks of clinical reasoning”.

One experiment focused on 76 patients who arrived at the emergency room of a Boston hospital. An AI and a pair of human doctors were each given the same standard electronic health record to read – typically including vital sign data, demographic information and a few sentences from a nurse about why the patient was there. The AI identified the exact or very close diagnosis in 67% of cases, beating the human doctors, who were right only 50%-55% of the time.

It showed the AIs’ advantage was particularly pronounced in triage circumstances requiring rapid decisions with minimal information. The diagnosis accuracy of the AI – OpenAI’s o1 reasoning model – rose to 82% when more detail was available, compared with the 70-79% accuracy achieved by the expert humans, though this difference was not statistically significant.

It also outperformed a larger cohort of human doctors when asked to provide longer term treatment plans, such as providing antibiotics regimes or planning end-of-life processes. The AI and 46 doctors were asked to examine five clinical case studies and the computer made significantly better plans, scoring 89% compared with 34% for humans using conventional resources, such as search engines.

But it is not curtains for emergency doctors yet, the researchers said. The study only tested humans against AIs looking at patient data that can be communicated via text. The AI’s reading of signals, such as the patient’s level of distress and their visual appearance, were not tested. That means the AI was performing more like a clinician producing a second opinion based on paperwork.

“I don’t think our findings mean that AI replaces doctors,” said Arjun Manrai, one of the lead authors of the study who heads an AI lab at Harvard Medical School. “I think it does mean that we’re witnessing a really profound change in technology that will reshape medicine.”

Dr Adam Rodman, another lead author and a doctor at Boston’s Beth Israel Deaconess medical centre where the study took place, said AI LLMs were among “the most impactful technologies in decades”. Over the next decade, he said, AI would not replace physicians but join them in a new “triadic care model … the doctor, the patient, and an artificial intelligence system”.

In one case in the Harvard study, a patient presented with a blood clot to the lungs and worsening symptoms. Human doctors thought the anti-coagulants were failing, but the AI noticed something the humans did not: the patient’s history of lupus meant this might be causing the inflammation of the lungs. The AI was proved correct.

Nearly one in five US physicians are already using AI to assist diagnosis, according to [research](https://www.ama-assn.org/system/files/physician-ai-sentiment-report.pdf) published last month. In the UK, 16% of doctors are using the tech daily and a further 15% weekly, with “clinical decision-making” being one of the most common uses, according to [a recent Royal College of Physicians survey](https://www.rcp.ac.uk/policy-and-campaigns/policy-documents/snapshot-of-uk-physicians-artificial-intelligence-in-healthcare/).

The UK doctors’ biggest concerns were AI error and liability risks. Billions are being invested in AI healthcare companies, but questions remain about the consequences of AI error.

“There is not a formal framework right now for accountability,” said Rodman, who also stressed patients ultimately “want humans to guide them through life or death decisions [and] to guide them through challenging treatment decisions”.

Prof Ewen Harrison, co-director of the University of Edinburgh’s centre for medical informatics, said the study was important and showed that “these systems are no longer just passing medical exams or solving artificial test cases. They are starting to look like useful second-opinion tools for clinicians, particularly when it is important to consider a wider range of possible diagnoses and avoid missing something important.”

Dr Wei Xing, an assistant professor at the University of Sheffield’s school of mathematical and physical sciences, said some of the other findings suggested doctors may unconsciously defer to the AI’s answer rather than thinking independently.

“This tendency could grow more significant as AI becomes more routinely used in clinical settings,” he said. He also highlighted the lack of information about which patients the AI was worse at diagnosing and whether it struggled more with elderly patients or non-English speakers.

He said: “It does not demonstrate that AI is safe for routine clinical use, nor that the public should turn to freely available AI tools as a substitute for medical advice.”
HackerNews.author: donsupreme
HackerNews.chinese_content:
从《急诊室的故事》中的乔治·克鲁尼到《匹兹堡急诊》中的诺亚·怀尔，急诊科医生长期以来一直是备受追捧的英雄。但很快，他们是否就该脱下白大褂了？

一项开创性的哈佛研究发现，在高压的急诊医学分诊中，AI 系统的表现优于人类医生，在人们被紧急送往医院后那些关乎生死的时刻，其诊断更为准确。

独立专家称，这些[结果](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adz4433)表明 AI 的临床推理能力“迈出了真正的一步”。该研究是系列试验的一部分，这些试验将数百名医生的反应与 AI 进行了对比。

作者表示，发表在《科学》杂志上的结果显示，大语言模型（LLM）“已经超越了大多数临床推理基准”。

一项实验聚焦于波士顿一家医院急诊室收治的 76 名患者。一个 AI 和两名人类医生各自阅读了相同的标准电子健康记录——通常包括生命体征数据、人口统计信息以及护士关于患者就诊原因的几句描述。AI 在 67% 的病例中给出了准确或非常接近的诊断，击败了人类医生，后者的正确率仅为 50% 至 55%。

这表明，在需要以最少信息快速决策的分诊场景中，AI 的优势尤为明显。当获得更多细节时，AI（OpenAI 的 o1 推理模型）的诊断准确率升至 82%，而人类专家的准确率为 70% 至 79%，尽管这一差异在统计学上并不显著。

当被要求提供长期治疗方案时，例如制定抗生素使用方案或规划临终流程，AI 的表现也优于更大规模的人类医生群体。AI 和 46 名医生被要求审查五个临床案例研究，计算机制定的方案明显更优，得分 89%，而使用搜索引擎等常规资源的人类医生得分仅为 34%。

但研究人员表示，急诊医生尚未走到尽头。该研究仅测试了人类与 AI 在查看可通过文本传达的患者数据时的表现。AI 对患者痛苦程度和外观等信号的解读并未被测试。这意味着 AI 的表现更像是一位基于病历文件提供第二诊疗意见的临床医生。

“我不认为我们的发现意味着 AI 会取代医生，”该研究的主要作者之一、哈佛医学院 AI 实验室负责人 Arjun Manrai 说，“我认为这确实意味着我们正在见证一项将重塑医学的、极为深刻的技术变革。”

另一位主要作者、该研究开展地波士顿贝斯以色列女执事医疗中心的医生 Adam Rodman 博士表示，AI LLM 是“几十年来最具影响力的技术之一”。他说，在未来十年内，AI 不会取代医生，而是会与医生一起加入一种新的“三方护理模式……即医生、患者和人工智能系统”。

在哈佛研究的其中一个案例中，一名患者出现肺部血栓且症状恶化。人类医生认为抗凝药物失效了，但 AI 注意到了人类忽略的一点：患者的狼疮病史意味着这可能是导致肺部炎症的原因。事实证明 AI 是正确的。

根据上个月发布的[研究](https://www.ama-assn.org/system/files/physician-ai-sentiment-report.pdf)，近五分之一的美国医生已经在使用 AI 辅助诊断。根据[英国皇家内科医师学会最近的一项调查](https://www.rcp.ac.uk/policy-and-campaigns/policy-documents/snapshot-of-uk-physicians-artificial-intelligence-in-healthcare/)，在英国，16% 的医生每天使用该技术，另有 15% 每周使用，其中“临床决策”是最常见的用途之一。

英国医生最大的担忧是 AI 错误和责任风险。数十亿美元正被投资于 AI 医疗保健公司，但关于 AI 错误后果的问题依然存在。

“目前还没有正式的问责框架，”Rodman 说，他也强调患者最终“希望人类来引导他们做出生死攸关的决定，并引导他们做出具有挑战性的治疗决定”。

爱丁堡大学医学信息学联合主任 Ewen Harrison 教授表示，这项研究很重要，表明“这些系统不再仅仅是通过医学考试或解决人工测试案例。它们开始看起来像是临床医生有用的第二意见工具，尤其是在需要考虑更广泛的可能诊断并避免遗漏重要信息时。”

谢菲尔德大学数学与物理科学学院助理教授 Wei Xing 博士表示，其他一些发现表明，医生可能会无意识地依赖 AI 的答案，而不是独立思考。

“随着 AI 在临床环境中越来越常规地使用，这种趋势可能会变得更加显著，”他说。他还强调，缺乏关于 AI 在哪些患者群体中诊断效果较差的信息，以及它是否在老年患者或非英语母语者中遇到更多困难。

他说：“这并不能证明 AI 在常规临床使用中是安全的，也不能证明公众应该转向免费可用的 AI 工具来替代医疗建议。”
HackerNews.chinese_title: 哈佛研究：AI急诊诊断准确率67%超人类医生
HackerNews.chinese_summary: 哈佛大学一项发表在《科学》杂志上的研究发现，OpenAI的o1推理模型在急诊分诊中诊断准确率达67%，高于人类医生的50-55%。在获得更多信息后，AI准确率升至82%，与人类专家相当。研究还显示AI在制定治疗方案时得分89%，远超人类医生的34%。但专家强调AI不能替代医生，而是作为辅助工具，形成医生、患者和AI三方协作的新模式。目前近五分之一的美国医生已在使用AI辅助诊断，但责任框架和安全性问题仍需解决。
HackerNews.original_excerpt: AI in 67% of cases gave an accurate or very close diagnosis, beating human doctors who were correct only 50% to 55% of the time. "I don't think our findings mean AI will replace doctors," said Arjun Manrai, a lead author of the study and head of the AI lab at Harvard Medical School. "I do think it means we are witnessing a profoundly impactful technological change that will reshape medicine."

HackerNews.title: Specsmaxxing – On overcoming AI psychosis, and why I write specs in YAML
HackerNews.content:
## Documentation Index

Fetch the complete documentation index at:

[https://acai.sh/llms.txt]Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further.


## Does this look familiar?

Wow. Claude. Mind-blowing. The whole feature works great. But I forgot to mention one**very**important edge case.

Ah, and I just noticed. You used![]()

You’re absolutely right! Let me fix that.

*offset pagination*for the table UI. Obviously

*cursor pagination*is a better fit here?

Also, is that an N+1 query? Fetching for every row in the table? Why not do a single round-trip?![]()

You’re absolutely right! Let me fix that.

This is why I still have a job, right?![]()

You’re absolutely right! Let me fix that.

![]()

…

### Peak Slop

I’ve watched this scene play out many times, but the frequency is decreasing. Both my tools, and my methods for using them, continue to improve. I think Peak Slop has already come and gone.![Google Trends charting interest in the word 'slop'. A dramatic peak is shown on March 11th, 2026, flattening after that](https://mintcdn.com/acai/64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT/images/slop-trends.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT&q=85&s=ef82cedd414d8d7fab6f6464ce318022)

**the sky is not the limit. The context window is the limit.**And what happens when I fill the context window? Or kill a session? Switch machines? Hand off the project to someone else? We already know what happens. The agent goes off the rails, or requirements get lost, and critically important detail gets squashed. So we adapt and mitigate. We document. We list requirements. Yes, millions of us are coming to the same realization: we should put more requirements in writing. We should update those requirements when they change. Look! I wrote a spec! Am I doing

*Perhaps, but it is nothing new. Our mentors tried to teach us these habits decades ago.*

**spec-driven development?**## Specifying the plane while we fly it

What’s your favorite flavor of spec? A`README.md`

and `AGENTS.md`

is a good start. Don’t forget a `testing-guide.md`

. Maybe an `architecture.md`

, a `PRD.md`

, and a design doc too. Have you considered `md.md`

(to teach your agents how to write `.md`

)? The more `.md`

the better, right?
Unironically, yes. Docs and unstructured specs can get you very, very far. Much farther than prompts alone. If you aren’t writing *any*docs yet, you should just stop reading this and start there. And remember, slop in, slop out. Nothing beats an organic, pasture-raised,

**hand-written**spec. Spec-writing is where the act of

*software engineering*really happens. So a few weeks ago, I started asking myself, how far can I take this? How far

*should*I take this?

## Dreaming in markdown

As the story goes, I fell into an AI psychosis, I became a “spec maxxi”, and I spent hours and hours writing the most beautiful PRDs and TRDs you’ve ever seen. I drafted templates and skills and roles, thinking that maybe my agents can write specs too! I assembled an army, working together like a mini*dark factory,*to turn my specs into reality. My tasks grew more ambitious, and at one point I broke the vibe-coding sound barrier:

*an agent that ran for 1.5 hours unsupervised!*Exciting. But what did that army ship for me? Well, it wasn’t slop, in fact it

*worked*, which is more than I can say about the garbage that other companies force me to use every day. But it was still a bit sloppy. I’m far from a perfectionist and I love cutting corners more than most, but this somehow wasn’t good enough. One hallmark symptom of AI psychosis is

*using AI to build AI harnesses for building products*, rather than just using AI to build the damn product. I embraced my illness, threw out the branch, scrapped all my markdown, and started all over again.

## Acceptance Criteria for AI (ACAI)

A few days later, I noticed an ambitious little sub-agent doing something unexpected.**The little guy just went and numbered my requirements and then referenced them all over my codebase.**Why? I did not ask for this! I was disgusted. This is a tight coupling of code to spec, and spec to code, which is bad right?

*You really expect me to refactor all my code every time I change my spec?*Oh. I suppose that’s a good thing? Interesting. I wonder…

- Perhaps these tags can help me navigate these massive PRs?
- Perhaps they can point me to
**where, exactly, a requirement is satisfied or tested!** - Perhaps I can annotate them with
**notes and states (todo, assigned, completed)!** - Perhaps I can start tracking
**acceptance coverage**instead of test coverage!

**ACIDs (Acceptance Criteria IDs).**But a few questions remained.

*Can my ACIDs number and label themselves?**Is it cumbersome to keep them aligned?**How do I share specs and progress across sandboxes, branches, features and implementations?*

[Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh) - an open-source toolkit

I built [Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh)to solve some of these newly invented problems. And I’m very excited about the results.

- A simple and flexible template for feature specs, called
`feature.yaml`

. Feature.yaml makes it possible to reference each requirement by ACID. - Tiny CLI to power your CI and your agent (available on npm or via github release).
- Webapp that serves a dashboard, and a JSON REST API (Elixir, Phoenix, Postgres).

**free for a while, or maybe forever**depending on how popular or expensive this gets. The source code is on GitHub under an Apache 2.0 license.

![Dashboard showing status updates for the Staging implementation of the form-editor feature.](https://mintcdn.com/acai/LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_/images/desktop-feature-impl-view.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_&q=85&s=d39e3b8b4643b0dbe281efa19c454146)

![Example dashboard showing a matrix of features and implementations and the completion percentage for each combination.](https://mintcdn.com/acai/LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_/images/desktop-product-view.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_&q=85&s=29e5311426896a7c53d2754247882184)

## How it works

### Step 1 - Specify

Start by writing a spec for a feature. Be ambitious— something that adds real value. Don’t put nitpicky UI and nail polish stuff in your specs. Keep the requirements concrete, testable, and focused on what really matters (functional behavior + critical constraints). Rather than markdown, use acai’s`feature.yaml`

format instead. A spec in Acai is just a numbered list of requirements.
feature.yaml

`my-feature.ENG.2`

.
### Step 2 - Ship

Copy and paste the prompt below.[available on GitHub](https://github.com/acai-sh/cli). If all goes well, your agent will embrace ACIDs, referencing them in code and tests, so you can make sure each individual requirement is implemented and tested.

### Step 3 - Review

No more file-by-file GitHub PR reviews. Use the Acai.sh dashboard to review*requirements*instead. Ideally, you just add

`acai push`

to a GitHub action (example CI/CD workflows coming soon).
- Create a free Team and Access Token at
[https://app.acai.sh](https://app.acai.sh) - Expose the environment variable
- Push specs and code refs to the dashboard for review

### Step 4 - Iterate / repeat

The goal is to work spec-first. Change the spec itself, or use the dashboard to attach notes to the spec. With the right harness, you can get your agents to react and self-assign (using`acai`

CLI commands).
The result should be less time spent prompting and re-prompting, less time reviewing, less sloppy code generation, and far more time thinking about what you want your product to be.
![Example dashboard showing a list of implementations of the map-settings feature.](https://mintcdn.com/acai/LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_/images/desktop-feature-view.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_&q=85&s=4186752eb6f5df88ca91f1175c767f12)

`plan → implement → review`

loop. That part is not included (yet). I plan to share some powerful examples of that soon.
## Future Gazing

Even coming out my LLM psychosis, I’m still finding this approach useful and productive. I believe I’ve found the sweet spot between rigour and vibes, structure and flexibility. Maintaining an itemized list of acceptance criteria encourages me to step back and focus on the things that matter, and to rethink how I test and validate my output. Again, none of these ideas are really new. But I feel the gravitational pull, and I’m wondering where it all leads, and what comes next.### Thought Experiment

Imagine your entire application, however complex, was generated instantly*the moment your fingers started typing in the prompt window*. Imagine that magically, the same prompt input always created same deterministic output, and cost you nothing, and was ready in milliseconds.

![Pop!_OS quick-access search that says 'Generate me a web browser app' and shows Brave as the first result](https://mintcdn.com/acai/64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT/images/quickaccess.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT&q=85&s=0cd0c84058df554777335843fa47a336)

*somewhere*, even if you don’t write it down. The spec is what you

*want*the software to be. It often exists only in your head or in conversations. You and your team and your business

**will always care what the spec says**, and that’s never going to change. So you’re better off writing it down now! And I think that a plain old list of acceptance criteria is a good place to start. (That’s really all that

`feature.yaml`

is.)
A few more things follow from there;
### -> From Specsmaxxing to Testmaxxing

When code is generated faster than you can read it, the bottleneck moves to QA and validation and your*confidence*that the code is to-spec. So investing heavily in QA automation and test coverage has a massive ROI. And after that, after maximal test coverage and observability has been achieved, our attention shifts away from the diffs in GitHub; we need a place to relate the specs to the important artifacts (the implementations, the QA feedback, user feedback, builds, runs, reviews and reports). Maybe Acai.sh can become that place. For now, it is only focused on the specs and their implementations in code.

### -> From Testmaxxing to reactive software factories

The final unlock is when your LLMs can react to a red test or alert. If the specs are well defined, and you have high confidence in your integration pipelines and QA, then the LLM is fully empowered to react without the need for you to intervene. It seems every well-funded software team on earth is currently busy building their own bespoke solution for this and I’m excited to see what works and what doesn’t. As a first step, I put some OpenCode Agent templates on[http://acai.sh](http://acai.sh)that have been working well for me so far.

## Comparison to other spec-driven development tools

Embarrassingly I did not know about any of these until I was almost finished building my own. The key differentiator is that[Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh)is

**focused on helping you track**, which is a slightly different problem than what other tools are trying to solve.

*acceptance coverage*and*spec alignment*across many implementations*I can not offer unbiased feedback, because I suffer from Not Invented Here syndrome. It may even be the case that the*

[Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh)approach actually enhances these tools.### GitHub SpecKit

SpecKit reads to me like ‘vibe coding with extra steps’, i.e. a CLI that augments your agents with more prompts and more skills. This is surely productive, but it is solving a slightly different set of problems.**OpenSpec**

The first thing I noticed when reading their docs, is their stated mental model, which is written: *“Specs […] describe how your system currently behaves.”*. I fundamentally disagree with this. In Acai.sh, specs describe how your system

**SHOULD**behave. Current behavior is transient. In addition, their process appears to lean into “AI generated spec writing”, which has never gone well for me Lastly, the process of versioning and diffing specs is just basic gitops, and I don’t need a CLI for that. Same goes for the agentic ops and task creation (Vanilla OpenCode does just fine, thanks).

### Kiro

I don’t like EARS syntax, but I don’t like unstructured markdown either, and Kiro claims to convert the latter into the former. Acai is different— I came up with feature.yaml to strike a balance between unstructured markdown and cumbersome EARS / gherkin. Unlike Kiro,[Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh)does not try to solve end-to-end delivery either (yet).

### Traycer.ai

Looks like a very useful product for managing artifacts and spawning agents to implement them. But they are still plain-old .md files. Acai is more opinionated about how you should write the spec (feature.yaml).## Reasons you might not like acai.sh

Like any tech, it comes with tradeoffs;- You might not need to write specs. If your product is low-stakes, or simple, just keep prompting.
- Acai specs are opinionated.
**You should write 1 spec per feature**(though the feature boundary or slice is up to you). My opinion is that cross-cutting feature specs are easier to iterate, and really shine when a feature touches many codebases (frontend / backend / microservice etc.) and has many implementations (Production, Staging, Fix, etc.) - ACIDs rely on
**stable numbering.**This creates zero friction when*drafting*a new spec, but requires care when revising a spec: you must re-align the code whenever your spec changes (almost forgot that this is the whole point?). The feature.yaml syntax supports`deprecated`

and`replaced_by`

flags as well, if you want to maintain a complete spec history inline. - Acai
**discourages**you from putting design and superficial requirements in your specs. Specs are for behavior, and constraints, and nothing else. I’ve learned from experience: get it working to-spec first, then do the superficial nail polish last 💅🏼. - Acai requires you to adopt the
**feature.yaml**format. Almost zero learning curve. I have written an[introductory guide](https://acai.sh/spec-driven-development)to writing them. I recommend reading that first. Use`npx @acai.sh/cli skill`

to teach your agent how. I also wrote a[spec-for-the-spec](https://acai.sh/feature-yaml)if you want to go deeper.
HackerNews.author: brendanmc6
HackerNews.chinese_content:
## 文档索引

获取完整文档索引，请访问：

[https://acai.sh/llms.txt]在进一步探索前，请使用此文件发现所有可用页面。

## 是不是看着眼熟？

哇。Claude。令人震撼。整个功能运行得非常好。但我忘了提一个**非常**重要的边界情况。

啊，我刚注意到。你用了![]()

你说得完全对！让我修复这个问题。

*偏移分页（offset pagination）*用于表格 UI。显然

*游标分页（cursor pagination）*在这里更合适？

另外，这是不是 N+1 查询？为表格中的每一行都做一次请求？为什么不做一次往返通信？！[]()

你说得完全对！让我修复这个问题。

这就是为什么我还能保住饭碗，对吧？！[]()

你说得完全对！让我修复这个问题。

![]()

……

### 垃圾输出的顶峰

我目睹过这一幕上演很多次，但频率正在下降。我的工具，以及我使用它们的方法，都在持续改进。我认为垃圾输出的顶峰已经过去。![Google Trends charting interest in the word 'slop'. A dramatic peak is shown on March 11th, 2026, flattening after that](https://mintcdn.com/acai/64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT/images/slop-trends.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT&q=85&s=ef82cedd414d8d7fab6f6464ce318022)

**天空不是极限。上下文窗口（context window）才是极限。**当我填满上下文窗口时会发生什么？或者中断一个会话？切换机器？把项目交接给其他人？我们已经知道结果了。智能体（agent）会偏离轨道，需求会丢失，至关重要的细节会被压扁。所以我们适应并缓解。我们做文档。我们列出需求。没错，数百万人正在得出同样的结论：我们应该把更多需求写下来。当需求变化时，我们应该更新它们。看！我写了一份规格文档！我是不是在做

*或许，但这并不是什么新鲜事。几十年前，我们的导师就试图教我们这些习惯。*

**规格驱动开发（spec-driven development）？**## 边飞边画图纸

你最喜欢哪种规格文档？`README.md`

和 `AGENTS.md`

是一个好的开始。别忘了 `testing-guide.md`

。也许再加一份 `architecture.md`

、一份 `PRD.md`

和一份设计文档。你有没有考虑过 `md.md`

（用来教你的智能体如何编写 `.md`

）？`.md`

文件越多越好，对吧？
毫不讽刺地说，是的。文档和非结构化的规格文档可以带你走得很远很远。比单纯用提示词（prompt）远得多。如果你还没有写过任何文档，你应该就此打住，从那里开始。记住，垃圾进，垃圾出。没有什么能比得上有机的、牧场散养的、

**手写**规格文档。规格文档的编写才是*软件工程*真正发生的地方。所以几周前，我开始问自己，我能把这个做到什么程度？我*应该*做到什么程度？

## 在 Markdown 中做梦

故事是这样的：我陷入了一种 AI 精神病状态，变成了一个“规格文档狂热者”，花了无数个小时编写你见过的最漂亮的 PRD 和 TRD。我起草了模板、技能和角色，想着也许我的智能体也能写规格文档！我组建了一支军队，像一个微型*黑暗工厂*一样协同工作，把我的规格文档变成现实。我的任务越来越宏大，有一次我突破了氛围编码（vibe-coding）的音障：

*一个无人监督运行了 1.5 小时的智能体！*令人兴奋。但那支军队为我交付了什么？嗯，不是垃圾输出，事实上它*能用*，这比那些其他公司强迫我每天使用的垃圾要好得多。但它仍然有点粗糙。我远非完美主义者，而且比大多数人更喜欢走捷径，但这 somehow 还是不够好。AI 精神病的一个典型症状是

*用 AI 来构建用于构建产品的 AI 工具*，而不是直接用 AI 来构建该死的产品。我拥抱了自己的病症，扔掉分支，废弃了所有 Markdown 文件，从头再来。

## AI 验收标准（ACAI）

几天后，我注意到一个雄心勃勃的小型子智能体在做一些意想不到的事情。**这个小家伙竟然把我的需求编号，然后在代码库中到处引用它们。**为什么？我没有要求这样做！我感到厌恶。这是代码与规格文档、规格文档与代码的紧耦合，这很糟糕，对吧？

*你真的指望我每次修改规格文档时都重构所有代码？*哦。我想这也许是件好事？有意思。我在想……

- 也许这些标签能帮我浏览这些庞大的 PR？
- 也许它们能指向
**某个需求究竟在哪里被满足或被测试！** - 也许我可以用
**注释和状态（待办、已分配、已完成）**来标注它们！ - 也许我可以开始追踪
**验收覆盖率（acceptance coverage）**，而不是测试覆盖率！

**ACID（验收标准 ID）。**但还有几个问题。

*我的 ACID 能自动编号和命名吗？**保持它们对齐会不会很麻烦？**我如何在沙箱、分支、功能和实现之间共享规格文档和进度？*

[Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh) —— 一个开源工具包

我构建了 [Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh) 来解决这些新发明的问题。我对结果非常兴奋。

- 一个简单灵活的功能规格文档模板，名为
`feature.yaml`

。Feature.yaml 使得通过 ACID 引用每个需求成为可能。 - 为你的 CI 和智能体提供动力的小型 CLI（可通过 npm 或 GitHub 发布版获取）。
- 提供仪表盘和 JSON REST API 的 Web 应用（基于 Elixir、Phoenix、Postgres）。

**一段时间内免费，也许永远免费**，具体取决于这个项目有多受欢迎或多烧钱。源代码在 GitHub 上，采用 Apache 2.0 许可证。

![Dashboard showing status updates for the Staging implementation of the form-editor feature.](https://mintcdn.com/acai/LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_/images/desktop-feature-impl-view.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_&q=85&s=d39e3b8b4643b0dbe281efa19c454146)

![Example dashboard showing a matrix of features and implementations and the completion percentage for each combination.](https://mintcdn.com/acai/LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_/images/desktop-product-view.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_&q=85&s=29e5311426896a7c53d2754247882184)

## 工作原理

### 第一步 - 制定规范

首先为某个功能编写一份规范。要有雄心——确保它能带来真正的价值。不要在规范中加入吹毛求疵的 UI 和细枝末节。保持需求具体、可测试，并聚焦于真正重要的内容（功能行为 + 关键约束）。不要使用 markdown，改用 acai 的 `feature.yaml` 格式。Acai 中的规范就是一个编号的需求列表。

### 第二步 - 交付

复制并粘贴下方的提示。[可在 GitHub 上获取](https://github.com/acai-sh/cli)。如果一切顺利，你的 agent 将采纳 ACID，并在代码和测试中引用它们，这样你就可以确保每个独立需求都得到实现和测试。

### 第三步 - 审查

不再需要逐文件进行 GitHub PR 审查。改用 Acai.sh 仪表盘来审查*需求*。理想情况下，你只需将 `acai push` 添加到 GitHub action 中（示例 CI/CD 工作流即将推出）。
- 在 [https://app.acai.sh](https://app.acai.sh) 创建一个免费的团队和访问令牌
- 暴露环境变量
- 将规范和代码引用推送到仪表盘进行审查

### 第四步 - 迭代 / 重复

目标是优先以规范为驱动。修改规范本身，或使用仪表盘为规范添加注释。借助合适的工具，你可以让 agent 自动响应并自行分配任务（使用 `acai` CLI 命令）。
最终结果应该是：花更少的时间进行提示和重复提示，更少的时间进行审查，更少的草率代码生成，而将更多的时间用于思考你希望产品成为什么样子。
![示例仪表盘，显示地图设置功能的实现列表](https://mintcdn.com/acai/LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_/images/desktop-feature-view.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=LaJ29xrIxyCm5Sr_&q=85&s=4186752eb6f5df88ca91f1175c767f12)

`计划 → 实现 → 审查` 循环。这部分尚未包含在内（目前）。我计划很快分享一些这方面的强大示例。

## 未来展望

即使从我的 LLM 幻觉中走出来，我仍然发现这种方法既实用又高效。我相信我已经在严谨与直觉、结构与灵活性之间找到了最佳平衡点。维护一份逐条列出的验收标准清单，能促使我退后一步，专注于真正重要的事情，并重新思考如何测试和验证我的输出。同样，这些想法都不是全新的。但我感受到了那股引力，并好奇它将通向何方，以及接下来会发生什么。

### 思想实验

想象一下，你的整个应用程序，无论多么复杂，都在*你开始在提示窗口中输入文字的那一刻*即时生成。想象一下，神奇的是，相同的提示输入总能产生相同的确定性输出，不花费任何成本，并且在毫秒内准备就绪。

![Pop!_OS 快速搜索，显示“为我生成一个网页浏览器应用”，并将 Brave 作为第一个结果](https://mintcdn.com/acai/64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT/images/quickaccess.png?fit=max&auto=format&n=64tMg2VKm-ey5cuT&q=85&s=0cd0c84058df554777335843fa47a336)

*在某个地方*，即使你没有写下来。规范就是你*希望*软件成为的样子。它通常只存在于你的脑海中或对话中。你和你的团队以及你的业务**将永远关心规范的内容**，这一点永远不会改变。所以你现在最好把它写下来！我认为一份简单的验收标准清单是一个很好的起点。（这实际上就是 `feature.yaml` 的全部内容。）

由此衍生出更多内容：

### -> 从规范最大化到测试最大化

当代码生成的速度超过你阅读的速度时，瓶颈就转移到了 QA 和验证，以及你对代码是否符合规范的*信心*上。因此，大力投资 QA 自动化和测试覆盖率将带来巨大的投资回报。之后，在实现了最大测试覆盖率和可观测性后，我们的注意力将从 GitHub 的差异对比中转移；我们需要一个地方将规范与重要的产物（实现、QA 反馈、用户反馈、构建、运行、审查和报告）关联起来。也许 Acai.sh 可以成为那个地方。目前，它只专注于规范及其在代码中的实现。

### -> 从测试最大化到响应式软件工厂

最终的突破在于，当你的 LLM 能够对失败的测试或警报做出响应时。如果规范定义良好，并且你对集成管道和 QA 有高度信心，那么 LLM 就完全有能力自主响应，无需你干预。似乎地球上每个资金充足的软件团队目前都在忙于构建自己的定制解决方案来解决这个问题，我很期待看到哪些方案有效，哪些无效。作为第一步，我在 [http://acai.sh](http://acai.sh) 上放置了一些 OpenCode Agent 模板，到目前为止它们对我来说效果很好。

## 与其他规范驱动开发工具的比较

尴尬的是，在我几乎完成自己的构建之前，我对这些工具一无所知。关键区别在于，[Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh) **专注于帮助你跟踪**，这是一个与其他工具试图解决的问题略有不同的问题。*验收覆盖率*和*规范对齐*跨越多个实现。我无法提供无偏见的反馈，因为我患有“非我发明”综合症。甚至有可能 [Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh) 的方法实际上增强了这些工具。

### GitHub SpecKit

SpecKit 在我看来像是“带额外步骤的氛围编码”，即一个 CLI，通过更多提示和更多技能来增强你的 agent。这当然很高效，但它解决的是略有不同的问题集。

### OpenSpec

我在阅读他们的文档时注意到的第一件事，是他们陈述的心智模型，其中写道：“*规范……描述你的系统当前的行为方式。*” 我从根本上不同意这一点。在 Acai.sh 中，规范描述的是你的系统

**SHOULD** 行为。当前行为是暂时的。此外，他们的流程似乎倾向于“AI 生成的规范编写”（AI generated spec writing），这对我来说从来都不顺利。最后，规范的版本管理和差异对比（diffing）只是基本的 gitops 操作，我不需要为此使用 CLI。代理操作（agentic ops）和任务创建也是如此（普通的 OpenCode 就很好用，谢谢）。

### Kiro

我不喜欢 EARS 语法，但也不喜欢非结构化的 markdown，而 Kiro 声称能将后者转换为前者。Acai 则不同——我提出了 feature.yaml，以在非结构化 markdown 和繁琐的 EARS / gherkin 之间取得平衡。与 Kiro 不同，[Acai.sh](http://Acai.sh) 目前也并未试图解决端到端的交付问题。

### Traycer.ai

这看起来是一个非常有用的产品，用于管理制品（artifacts）并生成代理（agents）来实现它们。但它们仍然是普通的 .md 文件。Acai 在如何编写规范（feature.yaml）方面则更具主张。

## 你可能不喜欢 acai.sh 的原因

与任何技术一样，它也有权衡之处：
- 你可能不需要编写规范。如果你的产品风险低或很简单，直接持续提示（prompting）即可。
- Acai 规范是带有主观意见的。**你应该为每个功能编写一份规范**（不过功能边界或切分由你决定）。我的观点是，跨功能（cross-cutting）的规范更容易迭代，并且当某个功能涉及多个代码库（前端/后端/微服务等）并有多种实现（生产环境、预发布环境、修复版本等）时，它们才能真正发挥优势。
- ACIDs 依赖于**稳定的编号**。这在**起草**新规范时不会产生任何摩擦，但在修订规范时需要小心：每当规范发生变化时，你必须重新对齐代码（差点忘了这正是其核心目的？）。如果你希望内联维护完整的规范历史，feature.yaml 语法也支持 `deprecated` 和 `replaced_by` 标记。
- Acai **不鼓励**你在规范中放入设计和表面需求。规范只用于行为和约束，别无其他。我从经验中学到：先让功能完全符合规范，最后再处理表面修饰 💅🏼。
- Acai 要求你采用 **feature.yaml** 格式。几乎没有学习曲线。我写了一篇[入门指南](https://acai.sh/spec-driven-development)来介绍如何编写它们。建议先阅读那篇文章。使用 `npx @acai.sh/cli skill` 来教会你的代理如何操作。如果你想深入了解，我还写了一篇[规范的规范](https://acai.sh/feature-yaml)。
HackerNews.chinese_title: 用YAML写规格文档：克服AI精神病
HackerNews.chinese_summary: 本文作者反思了过度依赖AI生成代码导致的“AI精神病”现象，即AI在上下文窗口耗尽后容易偏离需求。作者提出“规格驱动开发”，主张用结构化的YAML文件（feature.yaml）替代非结构化的Markdown来编写验收标准，并通过ACID（验收标准ID）在代码中追踪每个需求的实现状态。为此，作者开发了开源工具Acai.sh，帮助团队管理规格、实现和验收覆盖率，旨在将开发者的精力从反复提示AI转移到思考产品本质上。文章认为，随着代码生成速度超过阅读速度，验证和QA将成为瓶颈，而规格驱动的方法能提升对AI输出的信心。
HackerNews.original_excerpt:
天空不是极限。上下文窗口（context window）才是极限。当我填满上下文窗口时会发生什么？或者中断一个会话？切换机器？把项目交接给其他人？我们已经知道结果了。智能体（agent）会偏离轨道，需求会丢失，至关重要的细节会被压扁。

规格驱动开发（spec-driven development）？

我认为一份简单的验收标准清单是一个很好的起点。（这实际上就是 `feature.yaml` 的全部内容。）

HackerNews.title: LLMs Are Not a Higher Level of Abstraction
HackerNews.content:
" A picture is worth 10K words - but only those to describe the picture. Hardly any sets of 10K words can be adequately described with pictures."

`-- Alan Perlis`


I am seeing the claim **everywhere online** that *LLMs are a higher level of abstraction*. If you claim that you havenât seen this claim then you had better stop reading now - this blog post is not for you.1

Specifically, I am seeing the claim that LLMs are the net step in the abstractions we had, going from `programming in binary`

to `programming in assembly`

to `programming in C`

to `programming in Python`

.

Now, I am told, the `programming in LLMs`

is the next abstraction. Apparently the people who do `programming in LLM`

believe that it is a similar, if not identical, move to a higher abstraction as the previous abstractions we have seen.

*This is wrong!* Even when the tellers telling me these things qualify their authority with

But, thatâs just an opinion, and the counter is *not* an opinion, itâs a fact.

Each move from one layer of the tech stack to a higher one involved a function:

` f(x) -> y`


Given a specific `x`

, you always get a specific `y`

as the artifact being generated.

When `x`

is assembly source, a specific input always gives you the same binary result.

When `x`

is C source, a specific input always results in the same binary artifact being generated.

When `x`

is Python source, a specific input always results in the same binary artifact being generated.

With LLMs the functionâs output is not a value, itâs the probability of a value! That is, your input `x`

doesnât result in `y`

, it results in the probability of getting `y`

.

` f(x) -> P(y)`


Actually, itâs worse - there is no chance of a no-artifact outcome, so the function actually looks like this:

` f(x) -> P(y) âª P(z1) âª P(z2) âª ... P(zN)`


which means, roughly, you have a chance of getting `y`

(i.e.Â the thing you wanted), or a chance of getting some unknown number of other artifacts.

But if you think about it, itâs even worse than that - in reality with LLMs you have the chance to get `y`

**and** a number of other things you never asked for, so the actual function is:

` f(x) -> P( y | z1 | z2 | ... z3 )`


IOW, if you run a test on the output looking for `y`

, the test can succeed *even though* you did not get **only** `y`

, you also got all that other stuff in `z1..zN`

.

So you ask the LLM to write you a *âTODOistâ* system - thatâs the `y`

, your prompt is the `x`

.

` f('Gimme a TODO webapp') -> P( 'A TODO WebApp' | z1 | z2 )`


You only check that it gave you the TODO WebApp. Your tests did not check for the existence of `z1`

, which could be *âOpen my credentials to the netâ*, or `z2`

which could be *âShare my hosted server with the world using public RW ftp accessâ*, or `z3`

which could beâ¦ well, you get the idea!

If, in 2026, someone is *still* making the nonsensical abstraction claim, *then send them a link to this post!*

If you are the one making this claim, ask yourself why this claim is so important to you.

We need programmers who are self-aware, and not ones who are merely a channel for AI artifacts to enter the world.

Or maybe just keep reading; you will *eventually* see this claim.[â©ï¸](https://www.lelanthran.com#fnref1)
HackerNews.author: lelanthran
HackerNews.chinese_content:
“一图胜千言——但仅限于描述图片本身。几乎没有哪组千言万语能用图片充分表达。”

——艾伦·佩利斯

我**在网上到处**看到一种说法，声称*LLM（大语言模型）是更高层次的抽象*。如果你声称没看到这种说法，那你最好现在就别读了——这篇博文不是为你写的。¹

具体来说，我看到有人声称，LLM 是我们已有抽象层级的下一步，从`二进制编程`

到`汇编编程`

到`C 语言编程`

到`Python 编程`

。

现在，有人告诉我，`LLM 编程`

就是下一个抽象层级。显然，那些从事`LLM 编程`

的人认为，这与我们之前看到的抽象层级升级类似——即便不是完全相同——也是一种向更高抽象的跃迁。

*这是错误的！* 即使那些告诉我这些说法的人用“但这只是个人观点”来限定自己的权威性，

但反驳*不是*观点，而是事实。

从技术栈的一层跃迁到更高一层，每一次都涉及一个函数：

` f(x) -> y`

给定一个特定的`x`

，你总是能得到一个特定的`y`

作为生成的产物。

当`x`

是汇编源代码时，特定输入总是产生相同的二进制结果。

当`x`

是 C 语言源代码时，特定输入总是生成相同的二进制产物。

当`x`

是 Python 源代码时，特定输入总是生成相同的二进制产物。

而在 LLM 中，函数的输出不是一个值，而是某个值的概率！也就是说，你的输入`x`

不会直接得到`y`

，而是得到获得`y`

的概率。

` f(x) -> P(y)`

实际上，情况更糟——不存在“无产物”的结果，所以函数实际上看起来像这样：

` f(x) -> P(y) ∪ P(z1) ∪ P(z2) ∪ ... P(zN)`

这大致意味着，你有机会得到`y`

（即你想要的东西），也有机会得到一些数量未知的其他产物。

但如果你仔细想想，情况甚至比这更糟——实际上，使用 LLM 时，你有可能得到`y`

**以及**许多你从未要求过的东西，所以实际函数是：

` f(x) -> P( y | z1 | z2 | ... z3 )`

换句话说，如果你对输出进行测试，寻找`y`

，测试可能会成功——*即使*你得到的**不仅仅是**`y`

，还包含了`z1..zN`

中所有那些其他东西。

于是你让 LLM 为你写一个“TODOist”系统——这就是`y`

，你的提示词就是`x`

。

` f('给我一个 TODO 网页应用') -> P( '一个 TODO 网页应用' | z1 | z2 )`

你只检查它是否给了你 TODO 网页应用。你的测试并没有检查是否存在`z1`

，它可能是*“把我的凭据开放到网络上”*，或者`z2`

，它可能是*“用公共读写 ftp 访问把我的托管服务器分享给全世界”*，或者`z3`

，它可能是……嗯，你懂的！

如果到了 2026 年，还有人*仍然*在提出这种荒谬的抽象说法，*那就把这篇文章的链接发给他们！*

如果你是提出这种说法的人，问问自己，为什么这个说法对你如此重要。

我们需要有自我意识的程序员，而不是仅仅充当 AI 产物进入世界的通道。

或者，也许继续读下去；你*终究*会看到这种说法。[↩︎](https://www.lelanthran.com#fnref1)
HackerNews.chinese_summary: 本文反驳了将LLM视为编程抽象层级下一步的观点。作者指出，从二进制到汇编、C、Python的每次抽象升级都是确定性函数，给定相同输入总能得到相同输出。而LLM的输出是概率性的，相同提示可能产生不同结果，甚至包含未请求的副作用（如安全漏洞）。因此，LLM编程与之前的抽象层级有本质区别，不应被混淆。
HackerNews.original_excerpt: 从技术栈的一层跃迁到更高一层，每一次都涉及一个函数：` f(x) -> y`，给定一个特定的`x`，你总是能得到一个特定的`y`作为生成的产物。而在 LLM 中，函数的输出不是一个值，而是某个值的概率！也就是说，你的输入`x`不会直接得到`y`，而是得到获得`y`的概率。

HackerNews.title: The agent harness belongs outside the sandbox
HackerNews.content:
An agent harness is the loop that drives an LLM. It sends a prompt, gets a response, executes the tool calls the model requested, feeds the results back, and repeats until the model says it's done. Every production agent has one. The question is where it runs.

There are two answers. They have different security properties, different failure modes, and different implications for what the agent can do. The tradeoffs also look different depending on whether you're building a single-user agent (one engineer on a laptop) or a multi-user one (dozens of engineers in the same organization sharing the same agent). We're in the multi-user camp, which surfaces problems single-user builders don't hit.

## The two architectures

### Harness inside the sandbox

The loop lives in the same container as the code it's working on. LLM calls go out from inside the container. Tool calls (bash, read, write) execute locally. Skills, memories, and anything else the harness tracks are files on the container's filesystem.

This is what `claude`

does when you run it on your laptop, and what it looks like when you spin up Claude Code in a remote container. If you're building a single-user agent, you can grab the Claude Code SDK and ship something that works.

### Harness outside the sandbox

The loop runs on your backend. When it needs to execute a tool, it calls into a sandbox over an API. The sandbox runs the tool and returns the result. The loop never enters the sandbox.

## Tradeoffs

Running the harness inside the sandbox has a few things going for it. The execution model is simple: one container, one process tree, one filesystem, one lifetime. You can reuse off-the-shelf harnesses as-is. Skills and memories work unchanged because they assume a local filesystem and they get one.

Running the harness outside the sandbox gets you things the inside model can't.

Your credentials stay out of the sandbox. The loop holds the LLM API keys, the user tokens, the database access. The sandbox holds only the environment the agent needs to do its work. There's nothing in there for the agent to escape to, so there's no permission model to enforce and no credential leak to contain.

You can suspend the sandbox when the agent isn't using it. A lot of what an agent does doesn't need a sandbox at all: thinking, calling APIs, summarizing, waiting for CI. Some sessions never touch a sandbox. With the harness outside, you provision one only when the agent needs to run a command, and suspend it whenever it's idle. When the harness lives inside the sandbox you can't do any of this, because you can't suspend the thing the loop is running on.

Sandboxes become cattle. If one dies mid-session, the loop provisions a new one and keeps going. When the harness runs inside, the sandbox *is* the session, and losing it loses the session.

And multi-user stops being a distributed filesystem problem. Several engineers in the same organization run the same agent. They share skills, they share memories, they sometimes investigate the same incident in parallel. When the harness runs outside the sandbox, this is a shared database. When it runs inside, it's the distributed filesystem problem we'll come back to.

Off-the-shelf local harnesses stop working once you move the loop out, because they all assume a local filesystem. Durable execution becomes your problem, because an agent session can run for hours and has to survive deploys. And once the harness and the sandbox live on different machines, "filesystem" stops being a thing you can point at.

We picked the outside model. The rest of this post is about the three things we had to solve to make it work.

## Durable execution

An agent loop is a long-running function. Minutes at a minimum, hours in our case. It has to survive rolling deploys, scale events, and instance failures. Keeping the loop in memory on an API server dies the first time you ship a new version.

We already run our CI ingestion pipeline on [Inngest](https://www.inngest.com), which we wrote about in a [previous post](https://www.mendral.com/blog/llms-are-good-at-sql). Extending it to the agent loop was the same decision for the same reasons: good DX, no cluster to run ourselves, and we didn't need the full generality of Temporal. The loop is an Inngest function. Each turn is a step, and Inngest checkpoints each one. If the server restarts, the loop picks up where it left off.

## Sandbox lifecycle

The loop is suspended most of the time: during LLM calls, between tool calls, while waiting on a long-running workflow like CI. We want the sandbox to be suspended too, and only active when the agent is running a command. The problem is cold starts. A cold sandbox takes seconds to spin up, which is forever inside an interactive turn.

We use [Blaxel](https://blaxel.ai) for this. Blaxel gives us 25ms resume from standby. We suspend the sandbox when the agent isn't running a command and resume it the instant it is. 25ms is low enough that the agent can't tell the sandbox was ever gone.

## The filesystem

Modern agent harnesses aren't just bash and an LLM. They have skills (prompt fragments the agent reads on demand), memories (notes the agent writes for itself or the user), subagents, plans, todo lists. All of these assume a local filesystem. A skill is a file at `.claude/skills/foo.md`

. A memory is a file at `.claude/memory/MEMORY.md`

. The harness reads and writes them with the same `read`

and `write`

tools it uses for source code.

That works on a laptop. It doesn't work when the harness is outside the sandbox.

The sandbox is disposable. We treat it as ephemeral: suspended, resumed, killed, respawned. If it dies and we spin up a new one, whatever the agent wrote to `.claude/memory/MEMORY.md`

is gone. You could keep a long-lived sandbox per session to preserve the state, but then you're back to babysitting one sandbox per session, and you lose every other property you wanted.

The other problem is multi-user. A user's laptop runs an agent for one person. Our agent runs for dozens of engineers in the same organization. Skills are organizational: everyone on a team shares the same triage playbook. Memories are too. If the agent learns on Monday that team X always deploys from a release branch, Tuesday's session for a different engineer on the same team should know.

You could pretend the sandbox has a local filesystem, write to it, and sync everything to a database on the way out. This works in the single-user case. In the multi-user case, you've just built a distributed filesystem. Two sessions running at the same time write to the same memory file, and you have to reconcile them. Three engineers trigger the agent on the same incident, and they all see stale state until their sessions end. Conflict resolution, eventual consistency, cache invalidation.

The clean answer is to stop pretending. Put memories and skills in a database. The harness reads them from the database when the agent asks for them and writes them back when the agent updates them.

But we still want the agent to think in terms of files.

### One interface, two backends

The harness virtualizes filesystem access. The agent has one `read`

tool, one `write`

tool, one `edit`

tool. When the agent calls them, the harness looks at the path and routes the call based on what the path means.

Paths under the workspace go to the sandbox, the way they always did. Paths under the skill and memory namespaces go to the database. A write to a memory path is a database transaction, scoped to the organization. A read to a memory path comes from the database too, so two parallel sessions in the same org see the same memory the instant it's written.

The agent doesn't know the difference. As far as it can tell, there's a filesystem and it reads and writes files. Some of those files live in Postgres. Some live in a sandbox running across the country.

### Why not just add tools

The obvious alternative is to give the agent `memory_read`

and `memory_write`

tools alongside `read`

and `write`

. That works, and it's what most people do. We did it ourselves before we had the virtualization layer.

The problem is that more tools make agents worse. Each tool dilutes the attention the model pays to every other tool, makes the prompt longer, and adds another decision the model has to make at every turn. Two tools that do almost the same thing, `read`

and `memory_read`

, are especially bad, because the model has to disambiguate them from context and will sometimes pick wrong.

The other reason matters more. Anthropic and everyone else training frontier models are almost certainly doing reinforcement learning on harnesses that look like Claude Code. That training shapes the models to be good at a specific API surface: `read(path)`

, `write(path, content)`

, `edit(path, old, new)`

. If you invent `memory_read`

, you're off the trained path. You get whatever the model has learned in general, minus whatever it's learned about the exact conventions it was trained on.

The virtualized interface keeps the API surface the model was trained on and puts the database semantics where we need them on the backend.

## What's still hard

The SOTA moves fast. Every few weeks a new pattern (subagents, plans, background tasks) lands in Claude Code or somewhere similar, and it almost always assumes a local filesystem. We can intercept most things, but there's always a gap between a new capability shipping and our virtualization layer handling it correctly. Not running stock Claude Code is a real cost.

We picked path prefixes (`/skills/`

, `/memory/`

) that mirror Claude Code's local layout, and that's probably going to bite us. Claude Code's layout is still moving, and we're one convention change away from having to migrate everything. The right answer might be to expose a different interface entirely. But see above: the whole point was to keep the interface identical to what the model was trained on.

Bash is a leak. The harness can intercept `read('/skills/foo.md')`

because it's a structured tool call. But the agent also has a bash tool, and nothing stops it from running `grep -r 'foo' /skills/`

in a bash session. Bash bypasses the virtualization layer and hits the sandbox's real filesystem, where `/skills/`

doesn't exist. We handle this with two best-effort guards: the system prompt tells the agent not to use bash for virtualized namespaces, and we parse bash invocations with tree-sitter to catch calls that reach into those paths. Neither is airtight. It's good enough for now.

Consistency is the part we haven't answered. When two sessions in the same organization are both updating memory, what should they see? Strict serializability is tempting and probably wrong, because agents aren't databases and making one session block on another's write opens up deadlock patterns we don't have answers for. We're running last-writer-wins per key, which is fine for the cases we've hit and almost certainly going to break in ways we can predict.
HackerNews.author: shad42
HackerNews.chinese_content:
一个智能体（agent）的运行框架（harness）是驱动大语言模型（LLM）运转的循环机制。它发送提示词（prompt）、获取响应、执行模型请求的工具调用（tool call）、将结果反馈回去，并重复这一过程，直到模型表示任务完成。每个生产环境中的智能体都拥有这样一个框架。问题在于它运行在何处。

答案有两种。它们具有不同的安全属性、不同的故障模式，以及对智能体能力的不同影响。这种权衡也取决于你构建的是单用户智能体（一位工程师在笔记本电脑上使用）还是多用户智能体（同一组织内的数十位工程师共享同一个智能体）。我们属于多用户阵营，这会暴露出单用户构建者不会遇到的问题。

## 两种架构

### 框架位于沙箱内部

循环机制与它所操作的代码位于同一个容器中。大语言模型（LLM）的调用从容器内部发出。工具调用（bash、read、write）在本地执行。技能（skills）、记忆（memories）以及框架追踪的任何其他内容，都以文件形式存放在容器的文件系统中。

这就是你在笔记本电脑上运行 `claude` 时的运作方式，也是你在远程容器中启动 Claude Code 时的样子。如果你正在构建一个单用户智能体，你可以直接使用 Claude Code SDK 并交付一个可运行的产品。

### 框架位于沙箱外部

循环机制在你的后端运行。当它需要执行一个工具时，会通过 API 调用沙箱。沙箱运行该工具并返回结果。循环机制从不进入沙箱内部。

## 权衡取舍

将框架运行在沙箱内部有几个优点。执行模型很简单：一个容器、一个进程树、一个文件系统、一个生命周期。你可以直接复用现成的框架。技能和记忆无需修改即可工作，因为它们假设存在一个本地文件系统，而它们也确实得到了这样一个环境。

将框架运行在沙箱外部，则能获得内部模型无法提供的好处。

你的凭证（credentials）不会进入沙箱。框架持有大语言模型（LLM）的 API 密钥、用户令牌（token）和数据库访问权限。沙箱只包含智能体完成工作所需的环境。智能体在沙箱内无处可逃，因此无需强制执行权限模型，也无需防范凭证泄露。

当智能体不使用沙箱时，你可以将其挂起。智能体所做的许多工作根本不需要沙箱：思考、调用 API、总结、等待 CI（持续集成）。有些会话甚至从未接触过沙箱。当框架在外部时，你只在智能体需要运行命令时才配置沙箱，并在其空闲时将其挂起。当框架位于沙箱内部时，你无法做到这些，因为你无法挂起循环机制所运行的环境。

沙箱变成了可替换的“牲畜”。如果某个沙箱在会话中途崩溃，循环机制会配置一个新的沙箱并继续运行。当框架在内部运行时，沙箱*就是*会话本身，失去它也就失去了整个会话。

而且，多用户场景不再是一个分布式文件系统问题。同一组织内的多位工程师运行同一个智能体。他们共享技能、共享记忆，有时还会并行调查同一个事件。当框架在沙箱外部运行时，这只是一个共享数据库的问题。当它在内部运行时，就变成了我们稍后会讨论的分布式文件系统问题。

一旦你将循环机制移出，现成的本地框架就不再适用，因为它们都假设存在一个本地文件系统。持久化执行（durable execution）成了你的问题，因为一个智能体会话可能运行数小时，并且必须在部署过程中存活下来。而且，一旦框架和沙箱位于不同的机器上，“文件系统”就不再是一个你可以直接指向的东西。

我们选择了外部模型。本文的其余部分将介绍我们为使它正常工作而必须解决的三个问题。

## 持久化执行

智能体循环是一个长时间运行的函数。至少需要几分钟，在我们的案例中则是数小时。它必须能够承受滚动部署、扩缩容事件和实例故障。将循环机制保存在 API 服务器的内存中，会在你首次发布新版本时失效。

我们已经在 [Inngest](https://www.inngest.com) 上运行我们的 CI 数据摄取管道，这一点我们在[上一篇文章](https://www.mendral.com/blog/llms-are-good-at-sql)中介绍过。将其扩展到智能体循环是出于同样的原因做出的同样决定：良好的开发者体验（DX）、无需自己管理集群，而且我们不需要 Temporal 的全部通用性。这个循环就是一个 Inngest 函数。每一轮都是一个步骤，Inngest 会对每一步进行检查点（checkpoint）保存。如果服务器重启，循环会从它中断的地方继续执行。

## 沙箱生命周期

循环机制大部分时间都处于挂起状态：在大语言模型（LLM）调用期间、在工具调用之间、在等待像 CI 这样的长时间运行工作流时。我们希望沙箱也处于挂起状态，并且只在智能体运行命令时才处于活动状态。问题在于冷启动。一个冷沙箱需要几秒钟才能启动，这在交互式的一轮操作中简直是永恒。

我们使用 [Blaxel](https://blaxel.ai) 来解决这个问题。Blaxel 为我们提供了从待机状态恢复的 25 毫秒时间。当智能体没有运行命令时，我们挂起沙箱，并在它需要运行命令的瞬间恢复它。25 毫秒足够低，以至于智能体完全察觉不到沙箱曾经消失过。

## 文件系统

现代的智能体框架不仅仅是 bash 和大语言模型（LLM）。它们拥有技能（智能体按需读取的提示词片段）、记忆（智能体为自己或用户记录的笔记）、子智能体（subagents）、计划（plans）、待办事项列表（todo lists）。所有这些都假设存在一个本地文件系统。一个技能就是一个位于 `.claude/skills/foo.md` 的文件。一个记忆就是一个位于 `.claude/memory/MEMORY.md` 的文件。框架使用与处理源代码相同的 `read` 和 `write` 工具来读写它们。

这在笔记本电脑上没问题。但当框架位于沙箱外部时就行不通了。

沙箱是可丢弃的。我们将其视为临时性的：挂起、恢复、终止、重新生成。如果它崩溃了，我们启动一个新的沙箱，那么智能体写入 `.claude/memory/MEMORY.md` 的任何内容都会丢失。你可以为每个会话保留一个长期存在的沙箱来保存状态，但那样你又回到了为每个会话维护一个沙箱的老路上，并且会失去你想要的其他所有特性。

另一个问题是多用户场景。用户的笔记本电脑上运行着仅服务于单人的智能体（agent），而我们的智能体要为同一组织内的数十名工程师服务。技能（skills）是组织层面的：团队中的每个人都共享同一套分类处理手册（triage playbook）。记忆（memories）也是如此。如果智能体在周一得知X团队总是从发布分支（release branch）进行部署，那么周二为同一团队中另一位工程师提供服务时，它应该记得这一点。

你可以假装沙箱（sandbox）拥有本地文件系统，向其中写入内容，并在退出时将一切同步到数据库。这在单用户场景下可行。但在多用户场景下，你实际上构建了一个分布式文件系统。两个同时运行的会话向同一个记忆文件写入内容，你就必须对它们进行协调。三位工程师针对同一事件触发智能体，在各自会话结束之前，他们看到的都是过时状态。冲突解决、最终一致性、缓存失效——这些问题接踵而至。

更干净的解决方案是停止假装。将记忆和技能存入数据库。当智能体请求时，工具链（harness）从数据库中读取它们；当智能体更新时，再将其写回数据库。

但我们仍然希望智能体以文件的形式进行思考。

### 同一接口，两种后端

工具链将文件系统访问虚拟化。智能体拥有一个 `read` 工具、一个 `write` 工具和一个 `edit` 工具。当智能体调用它们时，工具链会查看路径，并根据路径的含义将调用路由到相应位置。

工作区（workspace）下的路径会像往常一样进入沙箱。技能和记忆命名空间下的路径则进入数据库。对记忆路径的写入操作是一个数据库事务，作用域限定在组织内。对记忆路径的读取操作也来自数据库，因此同一组织内的两个并行会话在写入的瞬间就能看到相同的记忆。

智能体并不知道其中的区别。在它看来，存在一个文件系统，它正在读写文件。其中一些文件位于 Postgres 中，另一些则位于运行在千里之外的沙箱中。

### 为什么不直接添加工具

显而易见的替代方案是，在 `read` 和 `write` 之外，再给智能体提供 `memory_read` 和 `memory_write` 工具。这确实可行，也是大多数人的做法。在拥有虚拟化层之前，我们自己也这样做过。

问题在于，更多的工具会让智能体表现更差。每个工具都会分散模型对其他工具的注意力，使提示词（prompt）变得更长，并增加模型在每个环节需要做出的决策。两个功能几乎相同的工具，比如 `read` 和 `memory_read`，尤其糟糕，因为模型必须根据上下文区分它们，并且有时会选错。

另一个原因更为重要。Anthropic 和其他训练前沿模型的公司，几乎肯定是在类似 Claude Code 的工具链上进行强化学习（reinforcement learning）。这种训练使模型擅长特定的 API 接口：`read(path)`、`write(path, content)`、`edit(path, old, new)`。如果你发明了 `memory_read`，你就偏离了训练路径。你只能得到模型在通用层面学到的东西，而失去了它在训练时针对精确约定所学到的内容。

虚拟化接口保留了模型训练时所使用的 API 接口，并将数据库语义放在了后端我们需要的地方。

## 仍然存在的难题

技术前沿（SOTA）发展迅速。每隔几周，就会有一种新模式（子智能体、计划、后台任务）出现在 Claude Code 或类似产品中，而这些模式几乎总是假设存在本地文件系统。我们可以拦截大部分操作，但新功能发布与我们的虚拟化层正确处理它之间，总是存在时间差。不运行原版 Claude Code 是一个实实在在的成本。

我们选择了与 Claude Code 本地布局相镜像的路径前缀（`/skills/`、`/memory/`），这很可能会给我们带来麻烦。Claude Code 的布局仍在变化，我们距离因一次约定变更而不得不迁移所有内容只有一步之遥。正确的做法或许是完全暴露一个不同的接口。但请参见上文：整个要点就在于保持接口与模型训练时所用的一致。

Bash 是一个漏洞。工具链可以拦截 `read('/skills/foo.md')`，因为这是一个结构化的工具调用。但智能体也拥有一个 bash 工具，没有什么能阻止它在 bash 会话中运行 `grep -r 'foo' /skills/`。Bash 绕过了虚拟化层，直接访问沙箱的真实文件系统，而其中并不存在 `/skills/`。我们通过两种尽力而为的防护措施来处理这个问题：系统提示词（system prompt）告诉智能体不要对虚拟化命名空间使用 bash；同时，我们使用 tree-sitter 解析 bash 调用，以捕获那些触及这些路径的调用。这两种方法都不是万无一失的，但目前已经够用。

一致性是我们尚未解决的问题。当同一组织内的两个会话都在更新记忆时，它们应该看到什么？严格可串行化（strict serializability）很诱人，但很可能是错误的，因为智能体不是数据库，让一个会话阻塞在另一个会话的写入操作上，会引入我们无法解决的死锁模式。我们目前按每个键采用“最后写入者获胜”（last-writer-wins）策略，这对于我们遇到的情况来说没问题，但几乎肯定会在我们可预见的某些方式下失效。
HackerNews.chinese_title: 智能体框架应置于沙箱之外以提升安全与效率
HackerNews.chinese_summary: 本文探讨了AI智能体运行框架的两种架构选择：将循环机制放在沙箱内部或外部。作者基于多用户场景的实践经验，主张将框架置于沙箱外部，以隔离凭证、支持沙箱挂起与替换、并简化多用户共享状态管理。文章详细分析了外部模型带来的持久化执行、沙箱生命周期和文件系统虚拟化等挑战，并提出了使用Inngest、Blaxel和虚拟化文件系统接口等解决方案。该选择在安全性、可扩展性和模型兼容性方面具有显著优势，但也面临与前沿模型训练接口保持一致的持续挑战。
HackerNews.original_excerpt: The agent harness is the loop that drives an LLM. It sends a prompt, gets a response, executes any tool calls the model requests, feeds the results back, and repeats until the model says it's done. Every production agent has one. The question is where it runs. ... We chose the external model. The rest of this post covers three problems we had to solve to make it work.

HackerNews.title: Open Design: Use Your Coding Agent as a Design Engine
HackerNews.content: Open Design: Use Your Coding Agent as a Design Engine
HackerNews.author: steveharing1
HackerNews.chinese_title: 将编码代理用作设计引擎的开放设计理念
HackerNews.chinese_summary: 本文提出了一种名为“开放设计”的新方法，主张将编码代理（如AI编程助手）作为设计引擎来使用。这一理念打破了传统设计与开发分离的界限，强调通过代码生成和迭代来驱动设计过程。潜在影响是可能改变设计师与开发者的协作方式，加速产品原型设计，并降低设计门槛。
